hero.title: Knowledge Base
hero.subtitle: The section explains the meaning of the project screens: what a metric means, where the data boundary sits, and why the same number can be read differently in different modes.

overview.title: Quick entry into the knowledge base
overview.subtitle: The top block serves two tasks: quickly identify which topic explains a specific term or number, and avoid mixing the knowledge base with result analysis, failure diagnostics, and the backend map.

factTable.title: Which question leads to which topic

meaning.question: A number, status, or mode on the screen is still unclear
meaning.answer: The starting pair is Models plus Time. Those topics explain how the daily scenario is assembled, where the trading day ends, and why the same term later appears across multiple reports.

branchesAndSplits.question: The same [[guide-home-policy|Policy]] shows different numbers on history
branchesAndSplits.answer: The pair Branches plus Train / OOS / Recent / Full explains which mode the trade belongs to, how history is split into training and evaluation parts, and why comparison must stay inside one slice.

features.question: The source of the entry signal needs to be understood
features.answer: Features and indicators shows the feature groups behind the model decision, while [[guide-home-pfi|PFI]] helps identify which features move the answer the most.

trust.question: The reason a report can be trusted needs to be understood
trust.answer: Data truthfulness plus Tests and barriers explain how the project separates the forecast from the future result, how it catches [[guide-home-leakage|future-data leakage]], and which checks stop false statistics before they reach UI.

nextAfterGuide.question: The term is already clear, but now the result or the root cause matters
nextAfterGuide.answer: After the knowledge base the route splits: Analysis is for comparing final numbers and strategy behavior, while Diagnostics is for finding the cause of a weak result.

steps.termsFirst: If the whole screen is unclear, the normal starting pair is Models plus Time: those topics explain the base day scenario, the trading window, and the day-close moment.
steps.behaviorThen: If the question is already about comparing modes, the next layer is Branches plus Train / OOS / Recent / Full: it explains why one [[guide-home-policy|Policy]] produces different numbers across historical slices.
steps.trustFinally: If the question is about trust in the result, the final layer is Data truthfulness plus Tests and barriers: that is where data boundaries, [[guide-home-no-trade|NoTrade]], [[guide-home-leakage|future-data leakage]], and system guards are explained.

pageBoundaries.title: What guide explains and what it does not

bullets.knowledgeBaseScope: Guide explains the meaning of terms, rules, and numbers. It exists so that the screen can be read without guesswork and without mixing the model layer, trading rules, and historical slices.
bullets.notRuntimeMap: Guide is not a runtime or backend-project map. If the question is already about projects, entrypoints, or storage, the owner page lives in the developer domain.
bullets.notFailureAttribution: Guide does not search for the reason of a concrete strategy failure. Once the term is clear but the problem remains, the route continues in Analysis or Diagnostics.

relatedContours.title: How guide connects to neighboring sections

bullets.analysisRole: Analysis shows the strategy outcome and regime behavior in numbers: where the strategy earns, where drawdown grows, and in which days [[guide-home-no-trade|NoTrade]] appears more often.
bullets.diagnosticsRole: Diagnostics shows the cause of a weak result: which filter cut the day, where the decision broke, and which regimes repeat the same problem.
bullets.developerRole: Developer explains backend owner layers: projects, reports, contracts, and guard rails. That route matters after the product-side meaning of the term is already clear.

header.title: Models
header.subtitle: Role of Daily, Micro, SL, and Total, their place in the signal, and how to read them on screen.

meaning.title: What it means
meaning.intro: A short map of the full chain.
paragraphs: The layers run in sequence: Daily -> Micro -> SL.
Daily sets the base day scenario, Micro works only inside a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]], and SL adds execution-risk assessment.
Total is the final answer of the whole chain.

rows: Sets the base day scenario: growth, sideways, or decline.
Shows the base scenario from which signal assembly starts.
Determines whether a sideways day contains micro-growth, micro-decline, or no local tilt.
Shows whether the sideways day has a local tilt.
Checks execution risk on top of an already assembled direction.
Shows how risky the already assembled signal is to execute.
Combines the Daily answer, the Micro-model outcome, and the execution-risk assessment into one result.
Provides the final answer used by trading logic.

reading.title: How to read it in the UI and metrics
reading.intro: How to read each layer on the statistics screen.

daily.title: Daily model
paragraphs: The Daily model sets the base scenario of the day. It is the layer that decides whether the system sees [[models-daily-up|growth]], [[models-daily-down|decline]], or a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]].

If Daily leaves the day in a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]], the chain passes it to the Micro model. If Daily already sees growth or decline, the next layer does not change that base scenario.

To judge Daily fairly, compare its result on the familiar part of history ([[models-daily-train|Train for Daily]]) with its result on new days ([[models-daily-oos|OOS for Daily]]). If it looks much better on familiar days, the layer may be too tightly fitted to the past.

rows: Daily sees a workable edge toward growth.
Daily does not produce a confident day direction. Inside such a day, the micro model may show micro-growth, micro-decline, or no local tilt.
Daily sees a workable edge toward decline.

paragraphs: The Micro model exists only for the days where Daily left a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]]. It shows [[models-micro-up|micro-growth]], [[models-micro-down|micro-decline]], or no local tilt.

logic.title: How it works logically
logic.intro: Where the final answer changes along the chain.
paragraphs: Daily sets the base scenario. If the day stays sideways, Micro adds a local tilt. Then the SL model evaluates execution risk and the chain produces Total.
Comparing Daily, [[models-day-micro|Daily + Micro]] and Total shows where the final answer changed: at the base scenario, at the micro tilt, or at execution risk.

The chain stays honest only if the calculation uses data that was already known at decision time. That boundary is what [[models-asof|as-of slice]] and [[models-causal-input|causal input]] are for. If later information leaks into the model, the meaning of downstream metrics breaks.

contract.title: Contract and limits
contract.intro: Each layer has its own responsibility boundary and its own set of prohibitions.

daily.title: Daily-model contract
paragraphs: The Daily model must rely only on [[models-causal-input|causal input]] and produce the base day scenario without post-fact hints.
If the layer looks much better on familiar history than on new days, the cause is usually in features, the history boundary between training and evaluation, or an overly tight fit to the past.

micro.title: Micro-model contract
paragraphs: The Micro model is only allowed inside the [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]]. It must not override a clear growth or decline scenario already chosen by the Daily model.
The layer only counts as useful if it improves the sideways subset and does so on enough [[models-micro-coverage|sideways-day coverage]].

sl.title: SL-model contract
paragraphs: The SL model runs on top of an already assembled direction and looks at the risk of [[models-sl-stop-loss|stop-loss]] before [[models-sl-take-profit|take-profit]].
Its responsibility is the execution-risk assessment of a ready signal. It must not invent a new direction from zero.

total.title: Final-signal contract
paragraphs: Total combines the Daily answer, the Micro-model outcome, and the execution-risk assessment into one result.
If the screen shows only one final block, the main reading should go through Total.

pipeline.title: Pipeline and data contract
paragraphs: The full calculation must use only the data that was already available at forecast time. Future knowledge must not reach the model before the window closes.
If later data enters the calculation, even pretty metrics stop being trustworthy.

checks.title: Which checks catch errors
checks.intro: These checks help separate real model value from pretty but false statistics.

daily.title: Daily-model checks
daily.why: If the base layer looks good only on familiar history, later assembly steps cannot fix that. They only hide a weak foundation.
daily.example: If Daily looks strong on familiar history but clearly weaker on new days, the first checks are features, the history boundary between training and evaluation, and whether the layer was too tightly fitted to the past.

micro.title: Micro-model checks
micro.why: Without [[models-micro-coverage|sideways-day coverage]], the micro layer can look stronger than it is simply because it was evaluated on a tiny subset.
micro.example: If the micro metric improved but ran on very few days, that is not enough reason to claim the whole pipeline improved.

sl.title: SL-model checks
sl.why: A risk layer is only useful while it really separates direction from execution risk instead of suppressing everything.
sl.example: If drawdowns fall after the SL-model step and useful days do not disappear en masse, the execution-risk assessment is adding value.

total.title: Final-signal checks
total.why: Total is what appears in the final numbers on the screen. If it is assembled incorrectly, the result is distorted no matter how good the separate layers look.
total.example: If Daily and the Micro model both look sensible but the final answer behaves oddly, inspect the merge into Total first.

pipeline.title: Pipeline and data checks
pipeline.why: If the calculation uses data the system could not know yet, the whole screen becomes doubtful rather than one local metric.
pipeline.example: If the model received a field that becomes known only after the day closes, a good result on new days no longer proves honest quality. The error happened before the forecast was even calculated.

header.title: BASE / ANTI-D branches
header.subtitle: Base execution mode and protective reversal mode for some risk days.

overview.title: Why branches exist
paragraphs: A branch is an execution mode for an already assembled model signal. [[branches-base|BASE]] keeps the original trade side, while [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] may flip it on some risk days.
[[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] turns on only where original execution looks more dangerous and the filters confirm that a reversal is appropriate.

rows: Executes the original side with no reversal.
May flip the trade side on a confirmed [[branches-risk-day|risk day]].
Useful when the reversal reduces risk better than plain execution.

terms.description:
BASE — The base mode without side reversal. It is the comparison point for protective branches.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

ANTI-D — The anti-direction branch. It may reverse the trade side if the risk day and guard filters agree that doing so is safer.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

overview.why: Without two branches, one final number does not show whether the protection truly helped or only changed execution by chance.
overview.example: If [[branches-base|BASE]] earns a bit more but [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] cuts the heavy tail in drawdown, the anti-direction branch adds real risk value rather than cosmetic variation.

base.title: BASE
paragraphs: [[branches-base|BASE]] is the base execution mode with no side reversal.
BASE is the comparison point. Without it, there is no way to tell whether ANTI-D improves the risk profile or merely changes the trade path.

terms.description:
Original direction — The trade side produced before branch logic: long for a positive scenario and short for a negative one.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

base.why: Every protective branch must be compared against BASE, otherwise it is impossible to separate true protection value from noise.
base.example: If a protective branch looks good in isolation but barely beats [[branches-base|BASE]] on drawdown and execution quality, the extra complexity is not paying for itself.

anti.title: ANTI-D
paragraphs: [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] is a protective execution branch for some [[branches-risk-day|risk days]]. It flips the side of an already assembled signal when original execution looks more dangerous.

ANTI-D does not search for a new market scenario and does not argue with the model about the day direction. It answers only the execution question: keep the original side or enter through [[branches-anti-direction|anti-direction]].

terms.description:
Risk day — A day the system considers dangerous by execution profile. Only such days may qualify for ANTI-D.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

ANTI-D scope — ANTI-D is applied before trade simulation and changes the entry side itself.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

anti.why: ANTI-D is only useful while it stays a targeted protection on risk days. If it starts flipping everything, the link between model signal and execution is lost.
anti.example: If the branch activates only on heavy-risk days and reduces drawdown without collapsing trade activity, that is a sign of useful anti-direction protection.

conditions.title: ANTI-D activation conditions
paragraphs: ANTI-D turns on only when all protective conditions pass together. If even one condition fails, the branch stays in [[branches-base|BASE]].
Anti-direction is a fail-closed protection. It only activates when risk is confirmed, the move range is sane, and the liquidation buffer is still wide enough.

rows: Without confirmed risk, no reversal is needed.
Filters out days that are too quiet or too extreme.
Blocks reversal when the risk buffer is too thin.

terms.description:
MinMove band — A guard that checks whether the expected move is neither too small nor too extreme for anti-direction execution.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

Valid leverage — ANTI-D is only allowed with a valid final leverage. A protective branch must not create a broken risk profile.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

conditions.why: These hard conditions fix the cause, not the symptom: they stop the branch from activating on noise and later pretending that randomness was useful protection.
conditions.example: If the day is risky but the liquidation buffer is too thin, the correct behavior is to fall back to [[branches-base|BASE]], not to attempt a hopeful reversal.

usage.title: How to read results
paragraphs: Read [[branches-base|BASE]] and [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] through the full metric set rather than one pretty number. [[branches-pnl|PnL]], [[branches-maxdd|MaxDD]], traded-day share, and skipped-day share should sit side by side.
ANTI-D is useful only when it improves the risk profile in a meaningful way. Higher profit with worse drawdown, liquidations, or execution quality does not make the branch automatically good.

terms.description:
MaxDD — Maximum equity drawdown. It shows the worst historical drop the branch went through.

What it shows:

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

Trade% / NoTrade% — The share of traded days and skipped days. It shows whether a pretty result came from shutting the strategy down too often.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

usage.why: One number hides cause too easily. A metric package shows whether the branch improved the risk profile or merely changed the return path at random.
usage.example: If [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] gives up a little [[branches-pnl|PnL]] but clearly wins on [[branches-maxdd|MaxDD]] without killing [[branches-trade-share|trade activity]], it may be the stronger branch as a risk decision.

header.title: Train / OOS / Recent / Full
header.subtitle: History is split so the page can show where the model learned, how it behaves on new days, what shape it is in now, and how stable it looks across the long archive.

overview.title: Why splits exist
paragraphs: One model result cannot be read honestly from a single slice. The page separates familiar history, new days, the recent window, and the full archive.

On this page those four slices are called [[splits-train|Train]], [[splits-oos|OOS]], [[splits-recent|Recent]], and [[splits-full|Full]]. One good result across the whole archive still does not guarantee the model looks equally good on fresh days.

rows: What was the model trained on?
How does it behave on new data?
Fresh tail of the available history.
How relevant is the model right now?
The full available history: train + oos.
How stable is it over a long horizon?

terms.description:
Recent — The last sliding window of history used to read current model form.

What it shows:
this term is used on the page as a reading anchor for the corresponding mode, metric, or calculation step. It makes visible which part of the logic or data flow is being discussed in the current block.

How to read it:
its meaning should be read together with the neighboring paragraph, table, or metric on the page rather than as an isolated label outside context.

Example:
if the nearby block shows a change in model quality, trade risk, or a time boundary, this term helps identify which layer of the calculation produced that change.

Full — The complete available history with both train and oos together.

What it shows:

train.title: Train

train.why: Train is useful for reading the learning result, but it does not show how the model behaves outside familiar history.
train.example: Strong [[splits-train|Train]] without confirmation on [[splits-oos|OOS]] means the model describes the past well, but that is not yet proof of quality on new days.

oos.title: OOS
paragraphs: [[splits-oos|OOS]] contains the new days after the [[splits-boundary|train boundary]] that the model did not see during tuning.
This is the slice that shows how the model behaves outside familiar history.

oos.why: OOS removes the easiest shortcut: here the model can no longer rely on days it already saw during tuning.
oos.example: If [[splits-oos|OOS]] collapses, the first suspects are the history boundary, future data leaking into the input, or a real regime change, not a pretty [[splits-train|Train]] score.

recent.title: Recent
paragraphs: [[splits-recent|Recent]] shows the fresh tail of the available history. This slice is about current form, not about the average across the full archive.
If recent is much weaker than full-history, older history is supporting a nice average while the current market has already changed more than the full sample reveals.

full.title: Full history
paragraphs: [[splits-full|Full]] is useful for long-horizon stability: rare regimes, long cycles, and the overall shape of the strategy across the whole archive.
But this slice must always be read together with [[splits-recent|Recent]]. A strong full-history result must not calm the reader if current form is already deteriorating on fresh days.

full.why: Full history is needed so a short local success is not mistaken for proven long-term stability.
full.example: If [[splits-recent|Recent]] is temporarily weak while [[splits-full|Full]] remains strong, the right response is to study the current regime more deeply, not to discard the model automatically.

header.title: Time and trading days
header.subtitle: How the project defines the trading day, the exit moment, and the historical time boundary.

overview.title: Trading day
paragraphs: The project's trading day follows the [[time-nyse-session|U.S. regular session]], not UTC midnight. That session defines entry, exit, and split boundaries for the day model.
The same UTC date can belong to different trading days in the project. For the model and reports, the NY business day matters more than the calendar date itself.

overview.example: The same UTC date can belong to different NY business days depending on the season. Both UI and calculation must rely on the trading day contract rather than midnight.

baseline.title: Baseline exit
paragraphs: [[time-baseline-exit|Baseline-exit]] defines the end of the same NYSE session window in which the forecast was opened. The daily label, the honest fact, and the split boundaries all depend on this moment.
It is tied to the real session close and therefore keeps the same meaning across training, backtest, and UI.

dayKeys.example: If split logic is built on [[time-entry-day-key|entry day]] instead of [[time-exit-day-key|exit day]], some days can fall into the wrong segment even though the calendar still looks reasonable.

weekend.title: Weekends and exclusions
paragraphs: NY weekends are excluded from the trading flow not for aesthetics, but for a stable time contract. The model should operate only on business days where its NYSE-based timing logic is actually defined.
If such days are not filtered out, the archive fills with entries that do not have a proper trading context. That pollutes train, weakens split honesty, and makes the UI harder to read correctly.

weekend.example: If NY weekends enter train without a valid session contract, the archive may look fuller, but the link between features and day movement becomes dirtier.

overview.title: How to read features
paragraphs: [[features-feature|A feature]] is a market signal available at forecast time. What matters is which part of the market it describes: impulse, backdrop, volatility, or regime.
[[features-pfi|PFI]] shows how much model quality drops without that information. It must be read together with the meaning of the feature, otherwise the number has no conclusion behind it.

rows: The larger the absolute drop, the more the model depends on that feature.
The relation between feature value and model score.
The sign shows direction of influence and the magnitude shows strength.
Difference of mean values between classes.
Shows which way the feature is shifted across outcomes.

testsPage.title: Tests and safeguards
testsPage.subtitle: Which checks stop pretty but false statistics from reaching the backtest, API, and UI.

paragraphs: Tests are a set of independent barriers that catch different error classes in data, time boundaries, architecture, and reports.

Checks are split by error type: some catch future data entering the input, others enforce basic integrity of numbers and sections, others keep layer boundaries, and another group checks whether the full calculation still reproduces.
tests-overview.why: A single smoke test shows only a generic failure. Split checks point to the actual contract that broke.
tests-overview.example: A failing architecture test means a broken dependency boundary, while a failing sanity check usually means invalid values or a broken report payload. These are different causes.

paragraphs: [[tests-sanity-core|Sanity checks]] stop empty or broken reports from looking valid. They verify ranges, required sections, [[tests-sanity-nan|NaN]], and [[tests-sanity-infinity|Infinity]].
If the data is missing, the system should fail explicitly. Replacing emptiness with a safe-looking value makes the screen dishonest.
tests-sanity.why: Broken data often looks plausible. If the system does not stop at the first broken contract, the problem becomes visible too late.
tests-sanity.example: If a probability arrives as [[tests-sanity-infinity|Infinity]], the correct behavior is to stop the computation with an invariant error. The wrong behavior is to quietly replace it with `0` and keep rendering.

truthfulnessPage.title: Data truthfulness
truthfulnessPage.subtitle: How the project separates an honest forecast from facts that became known later, and how that meaning reaches reports.

labels.truth-causal: What was known before the decision
labels.truth-structure: How the project keeps them separate

paragraphs: Data truthfulness is the contract that keeps separate what was known before the decision, what became known after the window, and how the final result reaches the report.
Forecast and fact should stay side by side without being mixed. The screen should show what the model knew before the trade and what happened later.

rows: [[truth-overview-causal|Causal snapshot]] cuts future-only fields and fixes the moment of data availability.
[[truth-overview-fact|Post-fact outcome]] is computed only after the window closes and never flows back into the decision.
A comparison between forecast and outcome.
[[truth-overview-report|ReportDocument]] stores already assembled meaning for API and UI.
The same meaning in API and UI.
[[truth-overview-trace|Traceability]] preserves source, generation time, and selected slice.
Source and freshness of the report.

truth-overview.why: If these stages are mixed, a pretty screen no longer proves an honest computation.

paragraphs: [[truth-causal-core|Causal layer]] is the decision path that uses only what was already known at entry time.

[[truth-causal-entry|EntryUtc]], [[truth-causal-asof|AsOfUtc]], and [[truth-causal-cutoff|DataCutoffUtc]] fix that knowledge boundary. If later information reaches the input, the forecast becomes dishonest before reports and charts even appear.
truth-causal.why: A broken knowledge boundary corrupts the whole computation, not a single metric.
truth-causal.example: If realized [[truth-causal-minmove|MinMove]] reaches the feature row before the forecast, even a strong [[truth-causal-oos|OOS]] is no longer trustworthy. That is not a strong model; it is a broken [[truth-causal-contract|causal contract]].

paragraphs: [[truth-omniscient-core|Omniscient layer]] is the factual path after the result window closes. This is where the project computes what really happened to the trade and how the day ended.

truth-omniscient.example: Showing forecast [[truth-causal-minmove|MinMove]] next to realized [[truth-causal-minmove|MinMove]] is correct only when the realized value is computed inside the [[truth-omniscient-core|omniscient layer]] after the window closes rather than feeding the live decision.

The UI should read a ready contract instead of guessing missing pieces. If the report or section is missing, that is an error, not an empty screen.

truth-delivery.example: If the backend did not return the required section, the correct behavior is a contract error. The wrong behavior is an empty table that pretends the strategy simply had nothing to trade.
